questions.question: Why can a distributed system fail even when average fleet load looks healthy?
options: A small number of hot keys can overload one owner path while the rest of the fleet is mostly idle.
Because distributed systems ignore hashing.
Because average load always predicts the worst case.
Because caches cannot serve repeated reads.
questions.explanation: Skew breaks systems through concentration, not just volume.

questions.question: What is the first useful distinction to make when a key gets hot?
options: Whether the hotspot is read-heavy, write-heavy, or both.
Whether the codebase uses tabs or spaces.
Whether the traffic comes from one laptop.
Whether the key is stored in JSON.
questions.explanation: Read and write hotspots need different mitigation paths.

questions.question: Why might replication help for a hot read key but not for a hot write key?
options: Reads can often be spread across replicas, while writes may still require one serialization owner.
Replication only works during office hours.
Writes are never cached.
Read traffic does not use networks.
questions.explanation: Replication helps when work can be served from many places safely.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with selective hot-key replication?
options: It spreads read load, but adds staleness and invalidation complexity.
It removes all cost from the system.
It guarantees no key will ever become hot again.
It makes admission control unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Replication is powerful, but correctness boundaries must remain explicit.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that a hot key is harming one owner disproportionately?
options: Owner-node saturation compared with the fleet average plus top-key QPS.
Only total global request count.
Repository star count.
Median line length in docs.
questions.explanation: The problem is localized concentration, not just total volume.

questions.question: Why is request coalescing useful during a hot-key cache miss?
options: It collapses many simultaneous fetches into one backend request, reducing stampede pressure.
It permanently removes all future traffic.
It forces every request to wait forever.
It only works for write-heavy counters.
questions.explanation: Coalescing protects the backend during miss storms.

questions.question: What is the clearest reason to isolate top tenants or keys onto dedicated capacity?
options: It reduces blast radius so one hotspot does not degrade unrelated tenants.
It guarantees lower hardware cost in every case.
It removes the need for any metrics.
It makes keys stop being popular.
questions.explanation: Isolation is a blast-radius control, not a magic efficiency trick.

questions.question: What is the strongest first clarification when asked about hot-key mitigation?
options: Ask whether the hotspot is temporary or sustained, and whether stale reads are acceptable.
Assume consistent hashing is sufficient.
Ignore correctness and only talk about scale.
Start by replicating every key.
questions.explanation: Mitigation choice depends heavily on duration and consistency tolerance.
